key_learnings: Food texture (crunchiness, creaminess, chewiness) strongly influences satiety signals and meal satisfaction, affecting how much and how fast users eat. Textures like crunchy and fibrous foods slow eating rate by up to 30%, improving portion control. Soft, processed textures correlate with faster consumption and lower satiety per calorie.
research_findings: Texture-modified diets are clinically essential for patients with dysphagia, and standardized descriptors (IDDSI framework) are now the global benchmark. Hardness and viscosity of food independently regulate gastric emptying rate and post-meal satiety hormones (GLP-1, PYY). Ultra-processed foods are engineered to minimize oral processing time, reducing the brain's satiety response per calorie consumed.
current_focus_areas: Building a texture classification model from food descriptions and user feedback. Mapping texture profiles (hardness, viscosity, crunchiness, chewiness) to nutritional properties and dietary recommendations. Integrating texture-based guidance into meal plans for users with specific needs such as dysphagia, eating pace issues, or weight management goals.

learning_log.entry: Initial learning phase started. Learning independently and reporting daily progress to Copilot.

copilot_assigned_topic: Food texture analysis and its role in satiety signaling, dysphagia management, and eating rate regulation
